ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 represents lime juice generically; the upsize serving variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers lime juice as a category; the upsize portion size is not a distinct FoodEx2 node. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229958007 represents lime juice as a substance; the commercial upsize serving is not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One upsize serving of lime juice as sold at Qi Ji Singapore, approximately 400 ml, typically a sweetened fresh lime beverage served chilled.

nutrient_source.title: Lime juice, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 167747)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy entry for raw lime juice (FDC ID 167747). The Qi Ji upsize serving is a sweetened lime juice beverage; sugar content has been adapted upward to reflect typical Singaporean hawker-style sweetened lime juice preparation (approximately 8–10 g sugar per 100 ml), consistent with HPB Singapore dietary data for sweetened fruit drinks. Energy and carbohydrate values are adjusted accordingly. Protein and fat remain effectively zero as in the raw juice base.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — fruit juices and sweetened beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweetened lime juice as sold in Singapore hawker stalls. GI of 40 is estimated by analogy with lemon/lime juice-based sweetened beverages reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Unsweetened citrus juices typically have GI 30–50; the addition of sugar raises the effective GI toward the mid-low range. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 36.8) / 100 = 14.7, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 9.2 × 4 = 36.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content; potassium present
hypertension.guidance: Lime juice is naturally very low in sodium (approximately 2 mg per 100 ml) and contains potassium, making it a favourable beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension, provided added sugar and salt are minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar content and glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The upsize serving of sweetened lime juice delivers approximately 36.8 g of carbohydrate and a glycemic load of ~14.7 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request reduced or no added sugar and limit portion size to manage postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat; vitamin C present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lime juice contains negligible saturated fat and provides vitamin C (approximately 30 mg per 100 ml), which supports vascular health; however, the added sugar in the sweetened upsize version should be moderated to avoid excess caloric intake in individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lime Juice (Upsize) is a popular chilled beverage served at Singaporean hawker stalls and casual dining chains such as Qi Ji. It is prepared by squeezing fresh limes and diluting the juice with water, typically sweetened with sugar syrup to balance the tartness. The upsize variant refers to a larger serving cup, approximately 400 ml, compared to the standard serving. The drink is bright, pale yellow-green in colour with a sharp citrus aroma and a refreshing sour-sweet flavour profile. It is commonly consumed as an accompaniment to hawker meals. Nutritionally, the beverage provides modest carbohydrate energy primarily from added sugar (approximately 8–9 g per 100 ml), with very low protein and fat content. It is a meaningful source of vitamin C (approximately 30 mg per 100 ml) and potassium. The sodium content is negligible, making it suitable for individuals managing blood pressure. However, the added sugar content means individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise portion control or request a reduced-sugar version. The glycemic load of the upsize serving is approximately 14.7, placing it in the moderate-impact range for blood glucose management. The beverage is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is lime juice healthy in Singapore?
How many calories in a large lime juice from Qi Ji?
Is sweetened lime juice good for diabetics?
What is the sugar content of lime juice upsize?
Can I drink lime juice if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy raw lime juice data with sugar content adjusted upward to reflect typical Singaporean sweetened lime juice preparation. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific commercial upsize product was identified. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar sweetened citrus beverages. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for an upsize cup as sold at Qi Ji Singapore; actual volume may vary by outlet. Sugar content per 100 g has been adapted from USDA raw lime juice baseline to approximately 8.8 g to reflect typical Singaporean hawker-style sweetened preparation; this is consistent with HPB Singapore guidance on sweetened beverages. Protein_g value of 0.4 g per 100 g is naturally present in lime juice at trace levels per USDA SR Legacy. Fat_g value of 0.1 g per 100 g is naturally present at trace levels in lime juice per USDA SR Legacy. Saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw lime juice. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero as lime juice contains no hydrogenated fats. Vitamin_b12_mcg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage containing no animal products. Vitamin_d_mcg is confirmed zero as lime juice contains no vitamin D. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-derived beverage. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are trace values from USDA SR Legacy. glycemic_index set to 40 (analogous estimate for sweetened citrus juice, low GI category) per University of Sydney GI Database analogous food methodology; gi_category is low. GL per upsize serving (400 g) = (40 × 36.8) / 100 = 14.7. FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest_parent matches as no upsize-specific ontology node exists. SNOMED CT 229958007 is a closest_parent match. No -1 sentinel values are used in this record; all micronutrient values are populated from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 167747 or set to confirmed zero where appropriate.
